coredata.dc:title: One-step ionothermal accompanied thermolysis strategy for N-doped carbon quantum dots hybridized NiFe LDH ultrathin nanosheets for electrocatalytic water oxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Zhiguo
Zhang, Deliang
Sun, Changhong
Song, Caixia
Wang, Debao
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we have developed a one-step ionothermal accompanied thermolysis strategy based on metal chloride deep eutectic solvent (DES) precursors to synthesize NiFe layered double-hydroxide ultrathin nanosheets hybridized with N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCD@NiFe-LDH). The NCD@NiFe-LDH hybrid shows hierarchical three-dimensional flower-like morphology assembled with ~1.4 nm ultrathin nanosheets possessing higher surface area values and well conductivity. The as-synthesized NCD@NiFe-LDH hybrid exhibits excellent performance for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and just require 363 mV to achieve a higher current density of 500 mA cm−2 compared to the state-of-the-art IrO2 in 0.1M KOH. Besides, NCD@NiFe-LDH hybrid shows long-term stability and satisfactory durability even at 1000 mA cm−2. Such an excellent OER performance at high current density can be ascribed to the unique porous ultrathin structure with abundant active edges and sites and the synergistic effect of the NCD and NiFe-LDH. The work offers a new way for the design and synthesis of other carbon-hybridized LDH ultrathin nanosheets with unique electrochemical properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Layered double hydroxides
N-doped carbon quantum dot
Deep eutectic solvents
Oxygen evolution reaction
high current densities
Hybrid material

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of NCD@NiFe‐LDH nanosheets by DES
Preparation of NiFe LDH‐H using hydrothermal method
Preparation of NCD@NiOOH and NCD@FeOOH by thermolysis of DES

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province
Taishan Scholar Project of Shandong Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by NSFC (52072194, 51872152) and the Natural Science Foundation (ZR2019BB065) and Taishan Scholar Program (Grant No. ts201712045) of Shandong Province of China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis procedure and morphology of the NCD@NiFe-LDH nanosheets.
(a) FE-SEM image; (b) (c) TEM image; (d) HRTEM image; (e) AFM image and (f) XRD pattern of NCD@NiFe-LDH.
N2 sorption isotherm and pore size distribution of NCD@NiFe-LDH.
XPS spectra of (a)full, (b) Ni2p, (c) Fe2p, (d) O1s, (e) C1s, and (f) N1s for NCD@NiFe-LDH.

(a) LSV curves, (b) Overpotentials with current densities of 10, 100, and 500 mA cm−2, (c) Tafel plots, (d) capacitive current density versus scan rate, and (e) Nyquist plots for NCD@NiFe-LDH, NiFe LDH-H, NCD@FeOOH, NCD@NiOOH, and IrO2 catalysts. (f) Durability test toward OER of NCD@NiFe-LDH.
(a) XRD pattern; (b) the XPS spectra of Ni 2p; (c) Fe 2p of NCD@NiFe-LDH after stability experiments.

head.ce:title: One-step ionothermal accompanied thermolysis strategy for N-doped carbon quantum dots hybridized NiFe LDH ultrathin nanosheets for electrocatalytic water oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (MOE), and College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (MOE)
and College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aKey Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (MOE), and College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Qingdao University of Science & Technology, Qingdao 266042, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bCollege of Materials Science and Engineering, Qingdao University of Science & Technology, Qingdao 266042, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Z. Hu and D. Zhang contributed equally to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we have developed a one-step ionothermal accompanied thermolysis strategy based on metal chloride deep eutectic solvent (DES) precursors to synthesize NiFe layered double-hydroxide ultrathin nanosheets hybridized with N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCD@NiFe-LDH). The NCD@NiFe-LDH hybrid shows hierarchical three-dimensional flower-like morphology assembled with ~1.4 nm ultrathin nanosheets possessing higher surface area values and well conductivity. The as-synthesized NCD@NiFe-LDH hybrid exhibits excellent performance for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and just require 363 mV to achieve a higher current density of 500 mA cm−2 compared to the state-of-the-art IrO2 in 0.1M KOH. Besides, NCD@NiFe-LDH hybrid shows long-term stability and satisfactory durability even at 1000 mA cm−2. Such an excellent OER performance at high current density can be ascribed to the unique porous ultrathin structure with abundant active edges and sites and the synergistic effect of the NCD and NiFe-LDH. The work offers a new way for the design and synthesis of other carbon-hybridized LDH ultrathin nanosheets with unique electrochemical properties.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid fossil fuels consumption and the greenhouse effect caused by the production of CO2 make people urgently develop sustainable and environmentally friendly fuels [1-3]. Among the candidates for future energy, hydrogen is regarded as one of the ideal energy carriers thanks to its high energy density and environmental protection characteristics [4,5]. Electrocatalytic water splitting is an effective strategy for producing hydrogen, but the OER process is the bottleneck in electrocatalytic water splitting, which limits the efficiency of the catalyst [6-8]. Although precious metals (such as IrO2 and RuO2) have high electrocatalytic activity, their rareness and high cost seriously hampers their industrial applications [9,10]. And also, industrial water electrolysis generally requires continuous operation at high current densities [11]. Therefore, developing low-cost, high-activity electrocatalysts is necessary to meet the needs of industrial development.

NiFe layered double hydroxide (NiFe-LDH) has synergistic effect between Ni and Fe species, which has good catalytic activity in alkaline electrolyte and is one of the most effective OER catalysts [12-14]. The active center of the OER process mainly occupied on the surface of the catalyst, so the size of the surface area may greatly affect the electrocatalytic performance [15,16]. In addition, it has been reported that the electrochemical properties of LDH strongly depend on their sheet thickness and surface properties [17,18]. However, the synthesis of NiFe LDH ultrathin nanosheets by traditional methods generally requires exfoliating, such as liquid exfoliation, solid-phase exfoliation, ultrasonication exfoliation, and so on [19-22]. For example, Wang et al. exfoliated CoFe LDH into ultrathin LDH nanosheets by Ar plasma etching [23]. Furthermore, the poor electrical conductivity restricts LDH to realizing its full potential in electrochemical catalysis. To this point, hybridization with conductive carbon materials (e.g., carbon quantum dots (CD)) has been confirmed to effectively improve the electrocatalytic properties of LDH due to their unique physical and chemical properties [24]. For example, Che and Kang's groups have reported that hybridization with CD plays an important role in improving conductivity and enhancing intrinsic activity of the LDH catalysts [25,26]. However, almost all the methods require the preparation of carbon quantum dots in advance and the following composite with LDHs. These methods are effective, however, searching for highly efficient route to synthesize CD hybridized LDH ultrathin nanosheet still remains significant for developing high performance electrocatalysts for OER at large current densities.

Deep eutectic solvent (DES) is a green solvent with the characteristics of safety, low toxicity, renewability, biodegradability, and low cost. It is a mixture system composed of at least two components with a melting point lower than any single component [27]. DES is currently used in the fields of organic synthesis, extraction, electrodeposition and material synthesis [28-30]. In the field of material synthesis, DES can be used as a solvent or directly calcined to obtain nanomaterials. Compared with the solid-phase method, the liquid-phase method can more effectively control the morphology of the materials, and materials usually contain more defects through this simple and quick synthesis strategy [31]. But there are few reports about the direct use of DES as a single precursor to prepare hybride nanomaterials via one-step ionothermal accompanied thermolysis.

Inspired by the above discussion, herein, we propose a simple one-step ionothermal accompanied thermolysis strategy to synthesize NiFe layered double-hydroxide ultrathin nanosheets hybridized with N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCD@NiFe-LDH). The DES precursor is directly synthesized into NCD@NiFe-LDH, in which ionothermal growing of NiFe-LDH ultrathin nanosheets is accompanied by in situ thermolysis of urea for N-doped carbon quantum dots. It shows advantages over those widely reported solvothermal/hydrothermal methods and ion-coprecipitation method, which usually require the preparation of carbon quantum dots in advance and the following composite with LDHs. By adjusting the composition of DES, ~1.4 nm NCD@NiFe-LDH ultrathin nanosheets can be facilely obtained. The obtained NCD@NiFe-LDH shows high specific surface area and unique pouous ultrathin structure with abundant active edges and sites. A smaller overpotential of 363 mV can be achieved even at a 500 mA cm−2 large current density compared to the state-of-the-art IrO2 in 0.1M KOH. And it also exhibits excellent stability and durability even at 1000 mA cm−2 in alkaline electrolytes. This method provides a feasible and environmentally friendly strategy for the design and synthesis of other carbon-hybridized ultrathin LDH nanosheets with unique electrochemical properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NCD@NiFe‐LDH nanosheets by DES
ce:section.ce:para: NCD@NiFe-LDH nanosheets were prepared by one-step ionothermal accompanied thermolysis of a four components DES. The detailed process is as follows: First, 0.01 mol NiCl2•6H2O, 0.003 mol FeCl3•6H2O, 0.08 mol urea, and 0.05 mol deionized water were evenly grinded and mixed and transferred a glass bottle, and then heated and stirred in a 60 °C oil bath for the sake of forming a uniform and clear liquid. The as-obtained DES liquid was then heated at 120 °C for 2 h in a Teflon-lined autoclave. After cooled and washed with deionized water, the NCD@NiFe-LDH nanosheets was achieved. By changing the amount of FeCl3•6H2O used to regulate the contents of Fe, the amount of FeCl3•6H2O in other experiments were 0.001 mol, 0.005 mol, and 0.007 mol for the preparation of NCD@NiFe-LDH-1, NCD@NiFe-LDH-5, and NCD@NiFe-LDH-7 catalysts, respectively. The experiments with different reaction temperature, reaction time, and the feed mass of urea in DES precursor were conducted following similar procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NiFe LDH‐H using hydrothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: NiFe LDH-H was prepared in a NiCl2-FeCl3-urea containing aqueous solution by hydrothermal method. The detailed steps are as follows: First, 0.01 mol NiCl2•6H2O, 0.003 mol FeCl3•6H2O, and 0.08 mol urea were evenly grinded and mixed and heated and stirred in a 60 °C oil bath forming a uniform and clear liquid, then slowly add 40 mL deionized water. The as-obtained solution was then heated at 120 °C for 2 h in a Teflon-lined autoclave. After cooled and washed with deionized water, the NiFe LDH-H was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NCD@NiOOH and NCD@FeOOH by thermolysis of DES
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of NCD@NiOOH is similar to that of NCD@NiFe-LDH. First, 0.04 mol NiCl2•6H2O, 0.08 mol urea, and 0.05 mol deionized water were evenly grinded and mixed and heated and stirred in a 60 °C oil bath forming a uniform and clear liquid. The as-obtained DES liquid was then heated at 120 °C for 2 h in a Teflon-lined autoclave. After cooled to room temperature, the sample was washed with deionized water to obtain NCD@NiOOH. The synthesis of NCD@FeOOH is similar to that of NCD@NiOOH with 0.004 mol FeCl3•6H2O.

The synthesis procedure of the NCD@NiFe-LDH nanosheets is shown in Fig. 1 , NiCl2•6H2O, FeCl3•6H2O, urea, and deionized water were mixed well in a 60 °C oil bath to form a uniform and transparent liquid (see Supporting Information, Fig. S1). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) spectrum were recorded to confirm that the as-formed NiCl2-FeCl3-urea-H2O four components liquid system is DES rather than a solution (see Supporting Information, Fig. S2 and S3). Then the uniform liquid was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and placed in an oven for ionothermal accompanied thermolysis reactions to obtain NCD@NiFe-LDH.

The morphology and microstructures of the as-synthesized NCD@NiFe-LDH were characterized by FESEM, TEM, and HRTEM images. The FESEM image in Fig. 2 a indicates that NCD@NiFe-LDH has a flower-like structure, which are constructed with nanosheets. TEM image in Fig. 2b shows the resulting nanosheets of the flowers with lots of wrinkled and folded edges. The high magnification TEM photograph in Fig. 2c reveals the nanosheets are almost transparent under the irradiation of the electron beam which implies the ultra-thin feature of the nanosheets. Some of the nanosheets are crimped or folded, from which the thickness of the nanosheets is estimated to be less than 2 nm (Fig. 2c). The irregular or uneven texture of the nanosheets hints the hybridization of NCD with NiFe-LDH and the porous structure. In addition, HRTEM image was performed on an individual nanosheet to investigate the detailed microstructure information. Fig. 2d shows the lattice fringes of the nanosheet are almost continuous as a whole displaying a high crystallinity of NCD@NiFe-LDH. Of note, some amorphous dots embed in the lattice fringes can be seen as indicated by the red circles in the image, which implies the presence of NCD in lower crystallinity and/or abundant pores or defects. The interplanar spacing of 0.20 nm is consistent with the (2 0 16) crystal plane of the LDH structure [29,30]. Further, atomic force microscopy (AFM) image has been recorded in order to determine the thickness of NCD@NiFe-LDH. As can be seen from Fig. 2e, the NCD@NiFe-LDH nanosheet exhibit an ultra-thin characteristic with a thickness of about 1.4 nm and a porous structure with serrated edges confirming the ultrathin nanosheets with abundant active edges and sites for electrocatalytic reaction. The formation of NCD@NiFe-LDH is also confirmed by X-ray diffraction result. XRD pattern in Fig. 2f indicates that the synthesized material has the structure of LDH (JCPDS#26-1286) [32,33]. But it is difficult to identify the characteristic peaks for carbon, perhaps due to its relatively low crystallinity or tiny grain size in NCD@NiFe-LDH hybrid. And, the formation of NCD was confirmed by Raman spectrum and the results are shown in Fig. S4, there are two obvious peaks at 1350, 1584 cm−1 attributing to the D and G bands in carbon materials, which indicates that carbon materials have been successfully introduced into NiFe LDH [34].

To get more structure information from NCD@NiFe-LDH ultrathin nanosheets, the N2 adsorption/desorption analysis was carried out. The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and pore size distribution curves are shown in Fig. 3 . It shows a type Ⅳ N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of an H3 type hysteresis loop indicating that NCD@NiFe-LDH has a mesoporous structure. From the isotherm, the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area was determined to be 226.7 m2•g−1. The pore size distribution was obtained by the BJH method from the adsorption branch of the isotherms and was shown in the inset of Fig. 3. It further confirms a mesoporous structure within sizes in the range of 3-10 nm which is consistent with the HRTEM results. Moreover, there are a great number of absorbed N2 at 0.9-1.0 high pressure range indicating the formation of abundant macropores by loosely assembled sheetlike units in the NCD@NiFe-LDH sample. Such a unique macro/mesoporous structure benefits the transmission and adsorption of ions in the electrolyte and provides more catalytic active sites, and finally, results in an enhanced electrocatalytic OER activity.

The formation of such a mesoporous structure of 1.4 nm NCD@NiFe-LDH ultrathin nanosheets can be attributed to the one-step ionothermal accompanied thermolysis of DES precursor. The whole NiCl2-FeCl3-urea-H2O DES liquid formed by hydrogen bonds can be regarded as a coordination Pan-molecule. The ionothermal accompanied thermolysis reactions of these Pan-molecules at molecular level results in ultrathin NCD@NiFe-LDH nanosheets, in which ionothermal growing of NiFe-LDH ultrathin nanosheets is accompanied by in situ thermolysis of urea for N-doped carbon quantum dots. For that no extra H2O exists in the precursor, the concentration of NH3 or NH4 + formed from the thermolysis of urea will be very high, which may intercalate and etch the NiFe double hydroxide to obtain NiFe LDH [35]. In the meantime, the catalytic in situ carbonization of urea by NiFe species results in the generation of N-doped carbon quantum dots. Owing to the 120 °C low reaction temperature, the formed N-doped carbon may have lower crystallinity. In addition, the DES liquid system has a higher viscosity, which makes the growth units avoid the influence of solvent during the growth process. And it is easier for ions intercalation between the layers so that the layers can be easily exfoliated resulting in ultrathin nanosheets. The liquid DES single precursor also provides a unique surface/interface energy for the in-situ growth of NCD@NiFe-LDH hybrid with defect-richly mesoporous structure.

For comparison, NiFe LDH-H had been synthesized by the hydrothermal method at 120 °C by adding 40 mL of water to the DES system. The added water breaks down and disrupts the DES system forming Ni2+ and Fe3+ aqueous solutions, in which the added H2O are free water instead of bonded water [36,37]. Therefore, just hydrothermal hydrolysis reactions of NiCl2-FeCl3-urea-H2O occur and no urea thermolysis takes place, and the synthesized NiFe LDH-H has a bulk structure and no ultrathin nanosheets were obtained. The XRD patterns and FESEM image of NiFe LDH-H are shown in Fig. S5. Moreover, NCD@NiFe-LDH samples have also been synthesized under different molar ratio of NiCl2•6H2O/FeCl3•6H2O, reaction temperature, and reaction time. The SEM images and XRD patterns of corresponding samples (Fig. S6-8). indicate that the molar ratio of NiCl2•6H2O/FeCl3•6H2O, reaction temperature, and reaction time have much influence on the phases, morphologies and microstructures of the samples. When the molar ratio of Ni:Fe is 10:3, the reaction temperature is 120 °C, and the reaction time is 2 h, NCD@NiFe-LDH ultrathin nanosheets with a mesoporous structure and high specific surface area can be facilely obtained. While for NCD@NiOOH and NCD@FeOOH prepared by thermolysis of DES, SEM images in Fig. S9 show that both of the samples consist of nanoparticles.

The chemical composition of NCD@NiFe-LDH was investigated by SEM EDX-mapping and the results were shown in Fig. S10. The element mapping confirmed the evenly distribution of Ni, Fe, O, C, and N elements in NCD@NiFe-LDH. The detailed surface electronic states of the synthesized NCD@NiFe-LDH was studied by XPS. The coexistence Ni, Fe, O, C, and N elements can be clearly seen from Fig. 4 a in the obtained materials, indicating the successful synthesis of a composite double metal hydroxide. The presence of tiny Cl element may be because of the intercalation Cl− in LDH. Fig. 4b shows Ni2p high-resolution spectrum of NCD@NiFe-LDH. Two peaks at 855.7 and 873.4 eV correspond to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2, respectively, associated with their satellite peaks. The banding energy difference between Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 is 17.7 eV, confirming the predominance of Ni2+ valence state [38]. The Fe 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 4c) also shows two main peaks, namely Fe 2p1/2 at 724.2 eV and Fe 2p3/2 at 712.5 eV, which are in good agreement with the characteristics of Fe3+ [39]. The O 1s high-resolution spectrum in Fig. 4d shows three fitted peaks. The peak at 530.8 eV comes from lattice metal-OH bond. The peak at 531.4 eV is attributed to hydroxyl group oxygen and the unsaturated oxygen species [40], suggesting NCD@NiFe-LDH were rich in oxygen vacancies. The other peak located at 532.5 eV arises from the adsorbed water relating to low oxygen coordination leading to high defect location in NCD@NiFe-LDH nanosheets. It is confirmed that these defect locations can beneficial to lower the overpotential for OER [41]. The C 1s high resolution spectrum is shown in Fig. 4e, which was fitted into four different carbon-containing peaks and can be assigned to those of C=N or C=O group, C−N or C−OH group, sp2 carbon atom C=C, and C−C group [42,43], respectively. For the N 1s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 4f), it can be fitted into two nitrogen-containing species of pyridinic N and pyrrolic N [42,43], indicating the doping of N atom in carbon. And most of them are pyridinic nitrogen atoms. As reported before, the presence of pyridinic-N for NCD can decrease the over-potential and enhance the electrocatalytic activity [44].The presence of C and N species on the surface of the composite confirms the successful synthesis of NCD@NiFe-LDH hybrid with NCD embedded in NiFe-LDH nanosheets. In addition, FT-IR spectra have also been recorded for NCD@NiFe-LDH to identify its composition. As shown in Fig. S11, there exist some CO3 2− and H2O in NCD@NiFe LDH. These anions of Cl− and CO3 2− should come from the intercalation in NiFe LDH. For comparation, XPS and FT-IR spectra for NiFe-LDH-H have also been recorded. As can be seen form Fig. S11 and S12, Cl−, CO3 2−, and H2O also can be identified in the sample, which should also come from the intercalation in NiFe LDH-H. It has been reported the interlayer anions and molecule (CO3 2− and H2O) in NiFe LDH can maintain the charge balance and stabilize the structure of LDHs [45].

The electrocatalytic activity of OER of NCD@NiFe-LDH was investigated by a standard three-electrode system with carbon cloth (CC) as a catalyst support. Firstly, the influence of molar ratio of Ni/Fe in the precursor, reaction temperature, and reaction time, and the feed mass of urea in DES precursor on OER activity of the product was studied. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves of different NCD@NiFe-LDH catalysts are shown in Fig. S13-16. The results display that the NCD@NiFe-LDH ultrathin nanosheets shows the lowest overpotential and therefore the highest electrocatalytic OER activity. In order to further reveal the nature of the electrocatalytic activity of NCD@NiFe-LDH ultrathin nanosheets, the OER catalytic activity of NiFe LDH-H, NCD@NiOOH, NCD@FeOOH, and IrO2 has also been studied for comparison. The LSV curves of these catalysts and the carbon cloth support is shown in Fig. 5 a. It can be observed that under the same overpotential, the NCD@NiFe-LDH catalyst exhibits a much higher current density than all the other catalysts. While bare carbon cloth support presents much lower OER activity. Fig. 5b shows a contrast of overpotentials at different current densities for different catalysts. It is clear that NCD@NiFe-LDH only needs overpotentials of 252, 311, and 363 mV to achieve 10, 100 and 500 mA cm−2current density, respectively, which are much lower than those of NiFe LDH-H (330, 453, 627 mV), NCD@NiOOH (426, 597, 966 mV), NCD@FeOOH (465, 711, 1157 mV), and IrO2 (312, 440, 612 mV). Moreover, NCD@NiFe-LDH needs only an overpotential of 401 mV to reach a high current density of 1000 mA cm−2. The Tafel slope was used to further assess the electrocatalysis reaction kinetics of different catalysts. The corresponding Tafel plots were acquired according to the Tafel equation, η = a + b log(j), which were obtained from the linear part plotting [46]. The NCD@NiFe-LDH catalyst clearly shows the better kinetics than those of NiFe LDH-H, NCD@NiOOH, NCD@FeOOH, and IrO2, as evidenced by its much smaller Tafel slope (Fig. 5c). The mesoporous NCD@NiFe-LDH ultrathin nanosheet exhibit excellent OER performance and just require 363 mV to achieve 500 mA cm−2 compared to the state-of-the-art IrO2 in 0.1M KOH. The OER activity is also much better than most of the lately investigated catalysts (as listed in Table S1). In order to exclude the different conductivity for NCD@NiFe-LDH and NiFe LDH-H, the experiments were carried out by adding some conductive carbon in NiFe LDH-H (NiFe LDH-H/C) to measure the electrochemical activity. It was found from Fig. S17 the electrochemical activity of NiFe LDH-H/C has not been greatly improved and is still much lower than that of NCD@NiFe-LDH, which further confirms the in-situ generated NCD is much superior to the added conductive carbon in enhancing the electrochemical activity.

Moreover, the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) was also a key index to confirm the electrocatalytic activity of catalysts and was estimated by determining the capacitance of double-layer (Cdl). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed with-various scan rates (Fig. S18) to get the Cdl capacitance currents of NCD@NiFe-LDH, NiFe LDH-H, NCD@FeOOH, NCD@NiOOH, and IrO2. And the Cdl values could be estimated from the linear slope of capacitive current density versus scan rate (Fig. 5d). It is verified that higher ECSA generally means more active sites. It is clear the Cdl value of NCD@NiFe-LDH (31.91 mF cm−2) is the highest among all the catalysts, which indicates that NCD@NiFe-LDH has much larger ECSA and much higher electrocatalytic OER activity [47]. The improved ECSA value of NCD@NiFe-LDH should be benefited from the novel mesoporous ultrathin nanosheet structure. In addition, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurements were performed to further study the electrocatalytic kinetics of NCD@NiFe-LDH catalysts and their corresponding Nyquist plots are shown in Fig. 5e. It is evidence NCD@NiFe-LDH shows a much smaller semicircle revealing well conductivity and faster charge transfer kinetics, which derived from the unique mesoporous ultrathin nanosheet structure with embedded N-doped carbon quantum dots.

Long-term stability and durability are also crucial to catalysts. Superior electrocatalysts not only have outstanding OER activity but also have fine stability and durability. The stability of NCD@NiFe-LDH was evaluated by continuous CV scanning performance. After 2000 cycles, the OER overpotential did not decrease significantly even at high current density of 1000 mA cm−2 (Fig. S19). The durability of NCD@NiFe-LDH was evaluated under different constant potentials by chronoamperometry. Fig. 5f shows the current densities of NCD@NiFe-LDH remain almost unchanged within 240 h. More importantly, NCD@NiFe-LDH remains highly stable with negligible decrease at large current densities (500 and 1000 mA cm−2) throughout 120 hours of continuous operation. These prove that NCD@NiFe-LDH has superior stability and durability under alkaline conditions.

Additionally, the structure and morphology of the catalyst after the stabilization experiment were tested by XRD, XPS, SEM, and EDX, and the results are displayed in Fig. 6 and Fig. S20-21. It can be seen that the XRD pattern (Fig. 6a) still meets the characteristic diffraction peak of LDH and SEM image (Fig. S20a) still shows a porous flower-like structure assembled with nanosheets. EDX-mapping (Fig. S20b-f) also indicates that the atoms compositions on surface also did not change significantly. Fig. 6b-c and Fig. S21 present the XPS spectra of NCD@NiFe-LDH after stability experiment to show the fine stability and durability. XPS analysis results of different atoms on the surface of NCD@NiFe LDH in origin and after stability experiment is shown in Table S2. It is evident the mole ratio of Ni/Fe does not change significantly and the element content of Ni and Fe just shows a slight decrease along with the increase of O content after stability experiment, which may result from the increase of oxygen species on the surface of NCD@NiFe LDH after long-time OER [48]. It is also noticeable Ni 2p and Fe 2p spectra in Fig. 6b-c show a slight shifted to higher binding energy (~ 0.2eV) in comparison with those in Fig. 4b-c, which may contribute to the surface oxidation of NCD@NiFe LDH after long-time OER [48]. All these results indicate that NCD@NiFe-LDH has almost no change in morphology and structure after stability experiment, which further proves its excellent stability and durability. Moreover, gas chromatography was used to further determine the OER Faraday efficiency of NCD@NiFe-LDH catalyst. Fig. S22 shows the theoretical value and the experimental oxygen evolution rate. The two values almost overlap, indicating that the OER Faraday efficiency of NCD@NiFe-LDH is almost 100%.

According to the above experimental results and discussions, the enhanced OER activity of NCD@NiFe-LDH can be attributed to its novel mesoporous ultrathin nanosheets structure with embedded N-doped carbon dots and the synergistic effect between Ni and Fe species. The mesoporous ultrathin structure and larger BET surface can expose more electrochemical active edges and sites. The hybridization of NCD and the highly crystalline nanosheet structure is favorable for electron transfer. The presence of pyridinic-N for NCD can decrease the over-potential and enhance the electrocatalytic activity Moreover, the meso-porosity of NCD@NiFe-LDH nanosheets assembly provides a buffer effect to avoid structure collapse of the nanosheets for long-term OER stability and endows higher surface area benefiting diffusion and adsorption of molecules to the surface of the catalysts.

In summary, a green, simple, low cost, and environmentally friendly DES based one-step ionothermal accompanied thermolysis strategy have been developed to controllably synthesize mesoporous NCD@NiFe-LDH ultrathin nanosheets. In this strategy, the DES single precursor is directly synthesized into NCD@NiFe-LDH ultrathin nanosheets. It shows advantages over those widely reported solvothermal/hydrothermal methods and ion-coprecipitation method, which usually require the preparation of carbon quantum dots in advance and the following composite with LDHs. By adjusting the composition of DES, the structure and morphology of NCD@NiFe-LDH can be easily controlled. Due to the mesoporous and ultrathin structure of NCD@NiFe-LDH and the presence of pyridinic-N, the synthesized ultrathin NCD@NiFe-LDH nanosheets acted as an efficient OER electrocatalyst. A small overpotential of 363 mV could be achieved even at a 500 mA cm−2 high current density. This strategy can provide new synthetic routes for design and synthesis of other carbon hybridized ultrathin LDH nanosheets with unique electrochemical properties.
Zhiguo Hu: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - Review & Editing
Deliang Zhang: Data Curation, Writing - Original Draft, Editing
Changhong Sun: Investigation,
Caixia Song: Conceptualization, Methodology,
Debao Wang: Supervision, Writing - Review & Editing

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by NSFC (52072194, 51872152) and the Natural Science Foundation (ZR2019BB065) and Taishan Scholar Program (Grant No. ts201712045) of Shandong Province of China.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2021.138932.
